title: Booms, Busts, and Common Risk Exposures
abstract: I present a dynamic general equilibrium model in which commonality in bank assets endogenously changes over the business cycle and shapes systemic risk. To reduce individual risks, banks diversify, increasing portfolio overlap and hence the similarity of their exposures to fundamental shocks. Systemic financial crises burst at the end of credit booms when productive investment opportunities are exhausted, banks' diversification incentives are strong, and their portfolios are highly correlated. A calibrated model is able to match key moments related to frequency, severity, and the economy's behavior around systemic crises.

article.introduction:
The global financial crisis of 2007–2008 has clearly shown thatinterdependent financial systems are subject to systemic risk. Prior to the crisis, a conventional view was that interdependent systems are resilient and can effectively absorb shocks hitting individual firms. Since the crisis, a growing body of research has argued that occasionally individual shocks can spread within interdependent systems, thereby exacerbating fragility. In this regard, crucial questions are how the economy evolves from states in which the financial system is robust to states in which it is fragile, and how systemic risk changes over time. This paper attempts to shed light on these questions.
  To this end, I construct a general equilibrium model featuring dynamic interactions between real aggregates, intermediaries' balance sheets, and financial interdependencies to study the endogenous evolution of systemic risk over time. The model's economy is granular—there is afinitenumber of sources of risk, or primitive assets (projects, for short). Banks invest in different primitive assets to diversify their individual risk exposures. Diversification is costly, that is, banks specialize in certain projects and have to incur monitoring expenses when investing in other projects. If all banks diversify simultaneously, their portfolios become overlapping and have similar exposures to underlying shocks. Because of common risk exposures, a negative project-specific shock might result in widespread defaults (i.e., a systemic crisis). Over the business cycle, time-varying credit supply and aggregate productivity govern the returns on the primitive assets, shaping banks' diversification needs and portfolio choices, the degree of portfolio commonality, and systemic risk.
  Systemic crises do not burst at random in the model. Lending expansions, driven by positive but temporary aggregate shocks, saturate the economy with credit and gradually exhaust productive investment opportunities. When positive shocks fade away, marginal products of the underlying projects are depressed because of decreasing returns to scale. Banks' investments generate low payoffs, and their profit margins narrow. To mitigate rising individual default probabilities, banks diversify more actively. Doing so helps them keep their individual default risks at relatively low levels but at the same time drives up the degree of portfolio overlap. Systemic risk is therefore high, and even moderate negative shocks to one or several underlying projects can cause simultaneous failures of many banks.
  These model predictions are consistent with recent empirical evidence. In the calibrated model, systemic crises are preceded by credit booms, a feature well established in the literature (Mendoza and Terrones (2008), Schularick and Taylor (2012)). Credit booms preceding systemic crises are initiated by positive shocks to total factor productivity; these shocks die out at the later stages of such booms, as documented by Gorton and Ordoñez (2020). In the model, systemic crises are also preceded by growing commonality in banks' exposures to underlying risks. Panel A of Figure1documents a gradual increase in the degree of overlap in U.S. banks' syndicated loan portfolios prior to the 2007–2008 crisis, indicating that in the precrisis years, banks were increasingly lending to firms operating in the same industries or located in the same states. Consistent with the model, this increase in portfolio overlap was associated with banks holding more diverse loan portfolios rather than focusing on particular industries or states (Panel B).1Importantly, the model is able to match many empirical features of systemic crises both qualitatively and quantitatively.
  To be more specific about the model, the economy consists of a long-lived representative household and the banking sector. The household owns all assets but can only access the production technology through banks. The household's role is to fund banks and make intertemporal asset accumulation decisions. The banking system is populated with ex ante identical short-lived institutions. All banks can invest in riskless storage. However, only some banks—referred to as investing banks—receive access to a set of more productive risky projects. Such heterogeneity gives rise to an interbank market in which investing banks borrow from noninvesting banks. Interbank debt is subject to costly default. Expected default losses, rationally priced in the interbank rate, can be reduced by diversification. Each investing bank specializes in one of the primitive assets and has to incur monitoring costs to invest in other projects. The optimal degree of portfolio diversification trades off monitoring expenses for a lower individual default probability and, hence, cheaper interbank debt.
  In normal times, the underlying projects are productive, and expected payoffs of investing banks' portfolios are high. Interbank debt is cheap because of low credit risk. The need for diversification is relatively small, and so the degree of portfolio commonality is low. Strong balance sheets and weakly correlated risk exposures of investing banks make systemic crises unlikely. The financial system is thereforerobust, that is, huge negative shocks are required to trigger a large-scale banking crisis.
  Systemic risk, however, increases at the end of credit booms. A typical credit boom is driven by a sequence of positive but temporary aggregate shocks that incentivize the household to accumulate more assets and provide more funding to banks. Banks then invest more in the decreasing-returns risky technology. As a result, at the end of credit booms, the risky technology generates below-average returns, tightening investing banks' profit margins. To mitigate the credit risk in the interbank market and prevent their borrowing rates from increasing sharply, investing banks diversify more actively. Diversification shifts their portfolio weights from projects of their specialization to a broader pool of assets. While it does help them keep their individual default probabilities at modest levels, the financial system as a whole becomesfragile: All investing banks are similarly affected by project-specific shocks, and even moderate negative surprises can make many of them insolvent at once.3
  In the model, large-scale banking crises, although painful, are a natural side effect of generally beneficial diversification. In the absence of diversification, investing banks' risk exposures are uncorrelated, and simultaneous defaults of many banks are very rare. However, banks frequently go bankrupt in isolation. Facing high individual insolvency risks, they borrow less and curb credit supply to the real economy. Thus, preventing banks from sharing risks and minimizing the number of systemic crises is suboptimal.4
  A natural question in this regard is whether the frequency of such events in a laissez-faire equilibrium is efficient. In the model, an individual investing bank fully internalizes how the degree of its portfolio diversification affects expected default costs borne by its lenders. Nevertheless, the decentralized allocation is characterized by too-frequent systemic crises. The source of inefficiency is a pecuniary externality in the presence of incomplete markets and real default costs (Greenwald and Stiglitz (1986)). Banks do not internalize that investments in the risky projects reduce their marginal productivities and therefore increase the probability of costly defaults. The representative household, in turn, does not internalize that savings lead the banking sector to receive more funding and invest more in the risky projects. As a result, the decentralized allocation is marked by overinvestment in the risky technology, inefficiently low returns, and an excessively fragile financial system.
  The model can be used to evaluate welfare impacts of financial innovations that ease diversification for financial institutions. Prior studies argue that financial innovations such as securitization and credit derivatives (e.g., credit default swaps and collateralized debt obligations) have facilitated diversification for individual institutions but at the same time intensified the degree of their common risk exposures and contributed to a lending boom (Shin (2009), Stulz (2010)). The model interprets such innovations as a reduction in diversification costs. This affects the economy in two ways. On the one hand, investing banks become more efficient in reducing their expected default losses. On the other hand, cheaper diversification expands investing banks' borrowing capacities and therefore drives up credit supply. As a result, the underlying projects' productivities are depressed further, and the adverse effects of the pecuniary externality are exacerbated. Such financial innovations in the model are destabilizing in the sense that they increase the number of systemic crises. However, the welfare implications are generally ambiguous and depend on whether diversification benefits outweigh the increased costs of the pecuniary externality.5
  I calibrate the model to match conventional macro moments as well as moments related to banks' profitability. An important aspect of the model is that it features two types of shocks: nondiversifiable aggregate shocks that hit all the underlying projects simultaneously and partially diversifiable project-specific shocks. To calibrate the magnitude of aggregate shocks, I use a standard approach based on matching empirical properties of Solow residuals. To calibrate the number of distinct risk sources and the size of project-specific shocks, I use postwar U.S. data on sectoral fluctuations for 41 nonfinancial industries. I find that seven principal components are able to explain the bulk of the overall variation, and around 30% of the overall variation can be explained by the first principal component alone. In the calibrated model, the number of major risk sources and the size of project-specific shocks are selected to match these two moments. Note that the fraction of variation explained by the first principal component is directly affected by the relative importance of aggregate fluctuations and thus is informative about the relative size of aggregate and project-specific shocks.
  Importantly, I do not use moments related to frequency or severity of financial crises as calibration targets. A natural way to evaluate the model therefore is to check whether it is able to match these untargeted moments. I simulate the calibrated model and find that it generates around 1.3 systemic financial crises per century. This is comparable to what is observed in the data from developed economies: According to the crisis chronology of Romer and Romer (2017), this number is 1.7 per century.6The model is also able to match well the size of credit and output boom-bust cycles around systemic financial crises. Investigating the importance of the pecuniary externality, I find that under a constrained planner that takes the underlying financial frictions as given but can affect the credit supply, the frequency of systemic crises is more than halved. Finally, I find that a decline in the cost of diversification from its benchmark value is welfare-reducing. Despite facilitating diversification, the fact that it also exacerbates the overinvestment problem turns out to be more important.
  Literature. The paper is related to two strands of literature. The first studies systemic risk in interconnected financial systems, primarily in static settings. The second strand incorporates financial frictions in macroeconomic models.
  The literature on financial links and fragility studies how shocks propagate within interconnected financial systems. Early contributions by Kiyotaki and Moore (1997a), Allen and Gale (2000), Freixas, Parigi, and Rochet (2000), and Lagunoff and Schreft (2001) present the first formal models of financial contagion. More recently, this literature has identified several major channels through which granular shocks might spread to the whole system and cause aggregate problems (see Cabrales, Gale, and Gottardi (2016) for a review). One is the domino effect: If institutions hold claims on each other, the failure of one organization might initiate a default cascade (Acemoglu, Ozdaglar, and Tahbaz-Salehi (2015), Glasserman and Young (2015)). Another channel involves common exposures to the same underlying assets, as in Elliott, Golub, and Jackson (2014) (their model also features the domino effect), Wang (2015), and Cabrales, Gottardi, and Vega-Redondo (2017). The common-exposure channel is at work in the papers emphasizing that banks investing in diverse assets become less risky individually but expose the financial system to a possibility of a large-scale crisis (Shaffer (1994), Acharya (2009), Stiglitz (2010), Wagner (2010and2011), Ibragimov, Jaffee, and Walden (2011)).7
  This paper's modeling of the banking sector is most similar to that of Cabrales, Gottardi, and Vega-Redondo (2017). However, whereas their paper analyzes financial structures emerging under a wide set of shock distributions, I allow for a richer shock structure but focus on a particular shock distribution. Moreover, banks in my setting endogenously choose their liabilities.
  More generally, in contrast to papers in this strand of literature, my paper presents a dynamic model in which banks' portfolios and systemic risk change over time. In principle, static analysis is able to reveal that financial systems are robust yet fragile (Haldane (2009)), that is, are susceptible to occasional systemic crashes if hit by sufficiently negative shocks. However, a dynamic setting is needed to analyze how systemic risk builds up over time and why shocks that are normally absorbed by the system can lead to large-scale crises in certain fragile states. Understanding the buildup phase of financial crises is crucial from the perspective of efficient systemic risk monitoring and timely policy interventions (Borio (2003), Adrian and Brunnermeier (2016)). Furthermore, using a fairly standard dynamic stochastic general equilibrium framework allows the model to be evaluated quantitatively.
  The second strand of literature, initiated by the classic works of Bernanke and Gertler (1989), Carlstrom and Fuerst (1997), Kiyotaki and Moore (1997b), and Bernanke, Gertler, and Gilchrist (1999), incorporates financial frictions into macroeconomic models. Many early papers rely on log-linear approximations around the steady state to examine the role of the financial accelerator for aggregate fluctuations. Nonlinear effects in economies with occasionally binding constraints are investigated by Mendoza (2010), He and Krishnamurthy (2012), and Brunnermeier and Sannikov (2014). In my setting, systemic crises are associated with painful recessions due to simultaneous collapses of many institutions. This is reminiscent of Gertler and Kiyotaki (2015), who study runs on the entire banking sector.
  Only a few recent papers attempt to explain boom-bust dynamics of credit around financial crises. In this respect, the works of Boissay, Collard, and Smets (2016) and Gorton and Ordoñez (2020) are most closely related. As in my paper, in their settings credit expansions reduce the marginal product of the underlying production technology. When it becomes sufficiently low, a crisis may occur, due to either agency issues (Boissay, Collard, and Smets (2016)) or production of information that reveals the collateral quality (Gorton and Ordoñez (2020); see also their earlier work, Gorton and Ordoñez (2014)).8Unlike these studies, my paper focuses on the interactions between credit expansions, banks' portfolio structure, and systemic risk. Another important difference is that, in my paper, crises are triggered by nonaggregate shocks, in line with the literature on the granular origins of aggregate fluctuations (Gabaix (2011)). This is reminiscent of the 2007–2008 financial crisis, which was driven largely by disastrous spikes in mortgage defaults in certain parts of the United States. As emphasized by Bernanke (2008), the rise in mortgage delinquencies and foreclosures during the crisis exhibited a strong regional pattern, with California, Florida, New England, and the upper Midwest being particularly hardly hit.
  The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. SectionsIandIIdevelop and characterize the model. SectionIIIassesses the model numerically and illustrates its dynamic properties. SectionIVconducts a welfare analysis. SectionVconcludes. TheAppendixcontains proofs and derivations omitted from the main text.
article.I. Model:
The model is infinite-horizon and discrete-time. Time subscripts are omitted and next-period variables are denoted by primes. Tildes indicate random variables subject to intraperiod uncertainty. The actors in the economy are a long-lived representative household and short-lived banks. The household owns all assets but can only access the production technology through intermediaries.
  The banking sector consists of a finite numberNof islands, whereN<∞$N<\infty$captures the degree of the economy's granularity and corresponds to the number of distinct risk factors. Each island is populated withM≤∞$M\le \infty$banks. Banks are risk-neutral, short-lived, and ex ante identical. At the beginning of a period, they raise the same amount of assetsao$a_{o}$from the representative household in the form of equity. All banks have access to a riskless storage technology with constant total returnρs$\rho _{s}$. Banks are ex post heterogeneous. In particular, in each period one institution per island becomes a representativeinvestingbank, and the remaining banks arenoninvesting. The two types of banks differ in their access to risky investment opportunities. Below, I first describe the real production side of the economy. I then discuss the interbank market.
  Although the storage technology is available to all banks, only investing banks have access to the risky projects. In particular, an investing bank from islandihas expertise in projectiand can invest in it at no cost. It can also allocate some of its assets to the remaining projects at an upfront proportional costf>0$f>0$per unit of investment, which includes any monitoring expenses. Noninvesting banks cannot access the risky technology directly. This heterogeneity gives rise to the intraperiod interbank market in which investing banks borrow from noninvesting banks.
  I assume that interbank borrowing happens within islands. This assumption implies that the economy features segmented interbank markets, where the interbank borrowing rate on islandiisρi$\rho _{i}$. This setup captures the idea that establishing new interbank links might be costly with these costs preventing cross-island borrowing. An island in the model includes banks with established relationships that can contact each other at no cost.
  Because investing banks invest in the risky technology, interbank debt is subject to defaults that are associated with deadweight losses. In particular, a fractionθ>0$\theta >0$of borrower assets is lost in the case of its default. This is rationally priced in the interbank borrowing rates. Therefore, despite their risk-neutrality and positive monitoring costs, investing banks are willing to invest in projects of other islands to diversify their individual risks and reduce the probability of costly default. As a result of cross-island investments, an investing bankiholds a portfolio{ωij}j=1N$\lbrace \omega _{ij}\rbrace _{j=1}^{N}$,∑j=1Nωij=1$\sum _{j=1}^{N}\omega _{ij}=1$, whereωij≥0$\omega _{ij}\ge 0$represents the fraction of its assets under managementai$a_{i}$invested in projectj. Here,ai$a_{i}$consists of assets raised directly from the household and borrowed in the interbank market. The upfront cost of portfolio formation isfai∑j≠iωij$fa_{i}\sum _{j\ne i}\omega _{ij}$.
  Portfolio weights{ωij}i,j=1N$\lbrace \omega _{ij}\rbrace _{i,j=1}^{N}$characterize commonalities in risk exposures across investing banks. In particular, a largeωij$\omega _{ij}$implies that investing bankiis strongly exposed to projectj's shocks. As long asωij>0$\omega _{ij}>0$∀i$\forall i$, a shock to projectjaffects all investing banks and can potentially lead to simultaneous defaults of many institutions. Importantly, defaults do not trigger cascading losses among investing banks because they do not hold assets directly related to the payoffs of other investing banks. I provide further discussion of the structure of the financial system at the end of this section.
  The problem of investing bankican now be formulated. Because the model is symmetric, island-specific indices are omitted where possible. Bankistarts a period with assetsao$a_{o}$that are raised from the representative household. Additionally, it borrowsab$a_{b}$at the rate ρ in the interbank market and chooses an investment portfolio{ωij}j=1N$\lbrace \omega _{ij}\rbrace _{j=1}^{N}$to maximize its expected payoff subject to a break-even constraint of its lenders.
  In equilibrium, the fraction1−μ¯1−μ$\frac{1-\bar{\mu }}{1-\mu }$of all assets in the economy is invested in the risky projects and the remaining share1−1−μ¯1−μ$1-\frac{1-\bar{\mu }}{1-\mu }$goes to the riskless storage technology. Throughout the paper, the risky projects are assumed to be sufficiently productive such that investing banks always want to borrow,μ>0$\mu >0$. This is indeed the case in the calibrated model.
  To summarize the description of the banking sector, Figure2shows its structure and gives the timing of the intraperiod events.
  The model's state variables are the stock of assetsA, aggregate productivityz, and project-specific shocksx=(x1,⋯,xN)$x=(x_{1},\dots ,x_{N})$,Ω=(A,z,x)$\Omega =(A,z,x)$. Because project-specific shocks are independent over time and are realized after banks have made their choices, a relevant set of state variables for banks isΩ−x=(A,z)$\Omega _{-x}=(A,z)$. I focus on symmetric equilibria. In any symmetric equilibrium, portfolio weights areωij=ωji$\omega _{ij}=\omega _{ji}$,ωii=ωjj=α$\omega _{ii}=\omega _{jj}=\alpha$,∀i,j$\forall i,j$. Under GHH preferences, there is no wealth effect on the labor supply, so wage does not depend onx. A decentralized recursive equilibrium consists of a set of quantities and prices described below.
  The financial system in the model is two-tiered. Noninvesting banks do not have direct access to the efficient production technology and lend their funds to investing banks. Investing banks then form diversified asset portfolios by investing in multiple projects. This is akin to a core-periphery structure of real-life financial systems (e.g., Boss et al. (2004), Craig and von Peter (2014), Craig and Ma (2022)).
  The assumption confining interbank borrowing within islands is not important for the results. Crucially, costly defaults happen when investing banks' payoffs drop below an insolvency threshold. Therefore, it is the structure of investing banks' asset portfolios that affects expected default losses and the interbank borrowing rate. The portfolio structure of noninvesting banks is irrelevant for expected default losses, so I simply assume that each noninvesting bank lends to one borrower.
  The main role of noninvesting banks is to provide funds to investing banks in the form of risky debt. Modern financial institutions are known to be financed to a large extent by debt. Moreover, funding through risky debt, especially short-term risky debt, was a crucial source of financial fragility during the crisis of 2007–2008 (Brunnermeier (2009)). The model is agnostic, however, about why debt arises as a source of funding in the first place. One possible reason is costly state verification (Townsend (1979)). When at least part of investing banks' funding comes from risky debt, banks have incentives to diversify to reduce their borrowing costs. Resulting asset commonality makes investing banks subject to similar shocks and gives rise to systemic risk. The source of such funding is of less importance. In particular, it can also come directly from households in the form of unsecured deposits.
  In the paper, investing banks hold overlapping asset portfolios. Asset commonality might arise both as a result of contractual links (e.g., when several banks initiate syndicated loans or participate in asset swaps) and in the absence of direct contacts (e.g., due to securitization or lending to similar firms). Systemic crises happen when many institutions have similar exposures to one or several badly performing underlying projects. Investing banks do not hold any securities directly related to payoffs of other intermediaries, for example, their debt or equity. As a result, defaults of investing banks do not cause default cascades—the domino effect—among other investing banks. Bankruptcy losses propagate from investing to noninvesting banks only within islands.
  As discussed in the review paper of Cabrales, Gale, and Gottardi (2016), the systems in which financial institutions have strong common risk exposures or are directly exposed to one another's defaults are susceptible to simultaneous failures of many institutions. Therefore, incorporating the domino effect into my setting is likely to make the financial system even more vulnerable to systemic collapse. Evidence on whether the domino effect is an important contributor to systemic risk is mixed (see Upper (2011) for a survey). Comparing relative strengths of the domino and the common exposures mechanisms in the Austrian banking system, Elsinger, Lehar, and Summer (2006) find that the latter is a dominant source of systemic risk. A crucial role of common exposures for systemic crises is also emphasized by Borio (2003).

This section outlines how banks' portfolio choices shape systemic risk, and how banks' decisions interact with the household's dynamic choices.

  The analyses above show how changes in the aggregate state variables affect the decisions of investing banks, the degree of portfolio overlap, and the probabilities of costly defaults. To emphasize the role of endogenous changes in the investing banks' portfolio structures for these outcomes, inInternet AppendixSectionVII, I consider the model in which investing banks are forced to invest only in projects of their islands. In that model, portfolio overlap is mechanically zero and systemic risk is low. At the same time, being unable to diversify their individual risks, investing banks face much higher individual default risks and much higher borrowing rates. As a result, they choose to borrow less in the interbank market (in particular, the constraintμ≤μ¯$\mu \le \bar{\mu }$is slack in more states of the world), resulting in lower investment in the productive risky technology.
  Even though the interbank problem is static, banks' optimal choices are functions of the state variables and thus change over time. This subsection discusses how the household's intertemporal asset accumulation choices and time-varying aggregate productivity interact with the interbank outcomes. In particular, it describes how the economy transits from states of low systemic risk to states of high systemic risk.
  Figure4shows equilibrium asset accumulation policiesA′(A,z,x)$A^{\prime }(A,z,x)$for two values of aggregate productivityz, a long-run averagezav$z_{av}$andzboom>zav$z_{boom}>z_{av}$, and two sets of realizations of project-specific shocks,xg<xb$x_{g}<x_{b}$(see caption for exact values). These policy functions are obtained from solving the calibrated model described in the next section. The discontinuity in the policyA′(A,zav,xb)$A^{\prime }(A,z_{av},x_{b})$atA*(zav,xb)$A^{\ast }(z_{av},x_{b})$is associated with a systemic crisis. In particular, forz=zav$z=z_{av}$andx=xb$x=x_{b}$, a systemic default happens ifA>A*(zav,xb)$A>A^{\ast }(z_{av},x_{b})$and does not happen otherwise (Corollary2). The discontinuity atA*(zav,xb)$A^{\ast }(z_{av},x_{b})$reflects the fact that if the economy starts the period withA>A*(zav,xb)$A>A^{\ast }(z_{av},x_{b})$,z=zav$z=z_{av}$, and the risky projects are hit by shocksxb$x_{b}$, all investing banks default simultaneously and a fraction θ of all assets in the economy is lost. That is, the project-specific shocks not only reduce the payoff to capital investors (5) directly but also lead to large real losses due to a systemic financial crisis. These default losses lead to sharp declines in the asset accumulation policy, consumption, and the household's welfare.14IfA<A*(zav,xb)$A<A^{\ast }(z_{av},x_{b})$, the same project-specific shocks lead to the same direct capital losses but do not trigger a systemic crisis, and so the economy is able to avoid large real default costs.
  Consider the economy starting at pointO, which is the steady state forz=zav$z=z_{av}$andx=xg$x=x_{g}$. At this point, the total amount of assets is relatively low and the risky projects are productive. Diversification needs are limited, investing banks' portfolio overlap is relatively low, and their risk exposures are not strongly correlated. In states with productive projects and low portfolio overlap, systemic crises are unlikely. Adverse realizations of project-specific shocks,x=xb$x=x_{b}$, bring the economy toBO$B_{O}$. This is not exceptionally painful because the financial system manages to avoid a systemic crisis and a large real loss of assets.
  Consider now the economy at pointC. Here, aggregate productivity is the same as atO,z=zav$z=z_{av}$. However, the amount of assets is significantly larger,AC>A*(zav,xb)>AO$A_{C}>A^{\ast }(z_{av},x_{b})>A_{O}$, which translates into a lower returnRand higher default probabilities of investing banks. Investing banks respond by more active diversification. Consequently, the degree of portfolio overlap increases and so does the commonality in risk exposures among investing banks. The probability of a systemic crisis is therefore higher than atO. The same project-specific shocksxb$x_{b}$that have only a modest negative impact on the economy atOare more detrimental atC. They cause large default losses due to simultaneous failures of all investing banks, bringing the economy fromCtoBC$B_{C}$.
  How does the economy move fromOtoC? A positive shock to aggregate productivity (in Figure4,zincreases fromzav$z_{av}$tozboom$z_{boom}$) drives up returns on the risky projects. The representative household starts to accumulate more assets, which leads to a credit expansion. However, due to its mean-reversion, aggregate productivity comes back tozav$z_{av}$after a few periods in the boom state. The economy arrives atCwhere it is more fragile than atO, in the sense that smaller project-specific shocks are able to cause a systemic crisis.
  The results of this section suggest that large-scale financial crises in this economy tend to occur after a period of credit expansion that is driven by a temporary productivity boom. In the following section, I calibrate the model and show that this prediction holds in the calibrated model.
article.III. Quantitative Analysis:
This section calibrates and quantitatively evaluates the model. Specifically, I explore how the model economy behaves around financial crises and how these model-implied behaviors compare to those in the data.
  The model period is one year. TableIlists parameter values. Some of the parameters are standard. Other parameters are calibrated to simultaneously match certain empirical moments. Below, I describe these moments and specify which parameters they are mostly helpful in identifying.15
  Preferences and production technology. Several parameters are set to the values commonly used in the macroeconomic literature. The time-discounting factor isβ=0.98$\beta =0.98$, the Frisch elasticity of labor supply isν−1=2$\nu ^{-1}=2$, and the intertemporal elasticity of substitution isψ−1=0.25$\psi ^{-1}=0.25$. I setδ=0.1$\delta =0.1$, which implies an average capital depreciation rate of 10%. For the risky production technology, I useη=0.3$\eta =0.3$, which implies an average capital share of 0.306. The small difference between the capital share and η arises because final output in the model can be produced via the risky production technology and via the storage technology. However, in the calibrated model, the storage technology is rarely used. The economy spends 68% of time in the states in which the storage technology is not used at all,μ=μ¯$\mu =\bar{\mu }$. On average, only 3% of the total capital stock is invested in storage. As a result, taking the storage technology into account when computing the capital share has a very small effect on the results.
  Aggregate shocks. Using postwar U.S. data on GDP, hours, and investment from the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA), I construct a series of Solow residuals and log-linearly detrend it. The same exercise is then repeated in the model. The parametersφz$\varphi _{z}$andσz$\sigma _{z}$are picked to match the persistence and the standard deviation of innovations in the AR(1) process (2). I obtainφz=0.90$\varphi _{z}=0.90$andσz=0.014$\sigma _{z}=0.014$.
  Project-specific shocks. Two parameters govern the importance of project-specific shocks: their volatilityσx$\sigma _{x}$and the number of independent risk factorsN. To parameterizeNandσx$\sigma _{x}$, I use U.S. data on sectoral fluctuations. In particular, I obtain data on value-added and stocks of private fixed assets for 41 private industries from BEA. The data cover the period from 1947 to 2018. I use the finest industry classification for which the data on value-added and capital stock are available for all industries starting from 1947. I exclude “Finance and insurance,” but the results are very similar if it is not excluded.
  For each industry, I construct real seriesVAj+(1−δ)Kj$VA_{j}+(1-\delta )K_{j}$, whereVAj$VA_{j}$andKj$K_{j}$are value-added and capital stock for industryjandδ=0.1$\delta =0.1$.16I detrend the series by using log transformation and HP-filtering them with a standard smoothing parameter of 6.25. I then employ principal component analysis (PCA) on the covariance matrix to identify the number of orthogonal factors that are able to explain the majority of the variation in sectoral fluctuations.17
  I use the fraction of total variance explained by the first principal component (around 30% in the data) to calibrate the standard deviation of project-specific shocksσx$\sigma _{x}$. In the model, the risky projects are symmetric and contribute equally to the overall variation. All of them are exposed to aggregate shocks. Therefore, the relative sizes of aggregate and project-specific shocks affect the importance of the first principal component in the model. In particular, if project-specific shocks are small relative to aggregate shocks, the first principal component accounts for a very high fraction of the overall variance; conversely, in the absence of aggregate shocks, the model would imply that all principal components explain the same share of the overall variance. Having already calibratedσz$\sigma _{z}$, I setσx$\sigma _{x}$so that the first principal component accounts for 30% of the overall variance when I repeat the same exercise as described above in the simulated data. The resulting value isσx=0.025$\sigma _{x}=0.025$.
  I set the number of major risk factorsNto seven. The first seven principal components account for a significant fraction of the overall variance (more than 80%). Each of the remaining components accounts for less than 3% of the overall variance. The choice ofN=7$N=7$is also justified by several popular factor selection criteria, such as Kaiser's rule (Kaiser (1960)) and parallel analysis (Horn (1965)). Kaiser's rule prescribes that only those factors whose eigenvalues are above one should be selected; intuitively, those factors are able to explain at least an average single series. This simple rule implies that 10 factors should be selected. The parallel analysis suggests that one should select only factors whose eigenvalues are above those obtained from uncorrelated normal variables. According to this criterion, five factors should be selected.18
  Importantly, the risky projects have the same size in the model. With symmetric projects, all principal components—besides the first one that reflects aggregate shocks—explain the same amount of variation. IncreasingNwould correspond to adding more projectsof the same size, which significantly improves the ability of investing banks to diversify risks. As a result, project-specific shocks would be substantially less likely to trigger widespread defaults in the banking sector. In reality, the factor corresponding to, say, the eighth principal component explains less than 3% of the overall variation. Such a factor is unlikely to be important for the ability of banks to reduce the riskiness of their portfolios through diversification.
  Banking sector. I set the diversification costf=0.0069$f=0.0069$to match the ratio of monitoring expenses to loan value reported by Craig and Ma (2022). Using the revealed preferences approach, they estimate that monitoring costs account for 23% of banks' expected gross loan value net of funding costs. More specifically, they consider a setting in which lending banks (noninvesting banks in my model) lend funds to borrowing banks through core intermediary institutions. The authors find that monitoring expenses are 23% of borrowing banks' loan values net of funding costs. In my framework, investing banks directly lend to the real sector, acting as a conglomerate of borrowing and intermediary organizations. When lending to projects outside of their own islands, they incur diversification costs. This reflects the idea that they need to pay an extra cost to verify payoffs of projects that they do not specialize in. The expected value of an interbank loan of sizeaisRa$Ra$, the funding cost isρa$\rho a$, and the amount spent on monitoring isf(1−α)a$f(1-\alpha )a$. The parameterfis calibrated so thatf(1−α)R−ρ$\frac{f(1-\alpha )}{R-\rho }$is on average 23%.
  The storage returnρs$\rho _{s}$and the number of banks per islandMare calibrated using balance sheets and income statements of the largest U.S. bank holding companies from the FR Y-9C filings. The storage return governs the cost of debt to investing banks ρ. The number of banks per islandMis related to the borrowing limitμ¯=M−1M$\bar{\mu }=\frac{M-1}{M}$. The parametersρs−1=0.05%$\rho _{s}-1=0.05\%$andM=570$M=570$are set to match the average net interest income over assetsR−ρμ$R-\rho \mu$(2.6% in the data) and the average interest income over assets net of interest expenses over liabilitiesR−ρ$R-\rho$(2.4% in the data).
  Finally, as in Bernanke, Gertler, and Gilchrist (1999), a fractionθ=0.12$\theta =0.12$of assets is lost in default.
  In the model, the economy is hit by aggregate and project-specific shocks. The goal of this subsection is to explore how the economy, being hit by these two types of shocks simultaneously, behaves around financial crises. To do so, I simulate the model for 500,000 periods. The incidences of investing bank defaults are then identified. I start by analyzing systemic crises, which from now on I define as events that occur when at least four investing banks collapse simultaneously.19Solid blue lines in Figure5show the average paths of both exogenous and endogenous variables around these events. Importantly, I do not feed any specific sequence of shocks into the model. The lines in Figure5take their shapes because they are conditioned on the event of a systemic crisis att=0$t=0$.
  Systemic financial crises do not hit the economy at random. In line with the predictions of SectionII, they tend to occur after booms in productivity and credit. In the beginning of a typical boom, a series of positive aggregate shocks incentivizes the representative household to save. Investment in the real economy grows. At this stage, the risky projects are still productive because of above-averagez. Diversification incentives are modest, and the degree of asset portfolio overlap across investing banks is close to its long-run average level.
  The probability of a systemic crisis increases sharply at the second stage of a boom. Just prior tot=0$t=0$—the period of a systemic crisis—aggregate productivity declines and even falls below its long-run mean. The stock of assets, being a slow-moving variable, stays high.20Whenzis low andAis high, the risky projects are less productive and deliver lower output per unit of investment. This narrows profit margins of investing banks, to which they respond by more active diversification. Their portfolios become more overlapping and more similarly exposed to the underlying shocks. At this point, if one or several projects are hit by bad shocks, many investing banks collapse simultaneously.
  As is evident from Figure5, below-average values of aggregate productivity contribute to elevated financial fragility just prior to systemic crises. At the same time, positive shocks tozare the drivers of credit booms preceding systemic crises. This is consistent with the findings of Gorton and Ordoñez (2020), who document that systemic crises are typically initiated by positive productivity shocks that subsequently die off.
  Unlike systemic crises, nonsystemic crises are marked by defaults of relatively few banks. Red dashed lines in Figure5show the average paths of the key variables around those events. Nonsystemic crises typically occur when portfolio overlap is below average and spreadR−ρμ$R-\rho \mu$is elevated. By Lemma2, narrower spreads make both systemic and nonsystemic defaults more likely ceteris paribus. However, investing banks respond to narrower spreads by allocating their assets more evenly among the underlying projects, which makes nonsystemic crises less likely. The second force dominates in simulations: 89% of all nonsystemic crises occur when portfolio overlap is below its long-run mean. In contrast, less than 3% of systemic crises occur under such circumstances.
  As in a textbook DSGE model, the variation in my model comes from exogenous shocks that shape the productivity of the real production technology, which in turn affects the representative household's decisions to consume and save assets intertemporally. The key novelty of the paper is to introduce a nontrivial banking sector. Recall that investing banks decide on their portfolio composition (captured by α) and on the amount of risky investment (captured by μ). To illustrate the importance of banks' endogenous reactions to exogenous shocks for the economic outcomes, in this subsection, I consider a counterfactual economy in which banks' portfolios are not time-varying.Internet AppendixSectionII.Cexplores the case in which investing banks always borrow up to capacityμ=μ¯$\mu =\bar{\mu }$such that the storage technology is never used.
  Specifically, I restrict α to be fixed at a long-run average level of the baseline model with flexible portfolios. This has two main effects on the economy. First, when investing banks are unable to adjust their portfolios, they are unable to control their default risks as effectively. As a result, the overall annual frequency of defaults increases from 3.11% in the benchmark model to 3.57% in the model with a fixed α. Second, crises become less severe on average. If portfolio compositions are fixed, investing banks are unable to reduce their individual risks when the underlying projects become unproductive. In response, they borrow less in the interbank market (constraint (10) is slack more often). Consequently, severe financial crises become less likely—portfolio overlap is never exceptionally high, and the risky projects receive less investment and are more productive on average. The annual frequency of simultaneous defaults of all investing banks decreases from 1.11% in the baseline model to 0.32% in the model with a fixed α.
  To further illustrate the importance of time-varying portfolio composition,Internet AppendixSectionVIIconsiders a model in which investing banks are forced to invest only in projects of their islands, so thatα=1$\alpha =1$. In that model, systemic crises are extremely rare because commonality in investing banks' shock exposures is mechanically low. At the same time, being unable to diversify their individual risks, investing banks substantially reduce their borrowing in the interbank market, resulting in much less resources being allocated to the productive risky technology and thus lower output and consumption.
  As discussed above, none of the calibration targets involves moments related to financial crises in general and their frequencies in particular. In this subsection, I investigate whether the calibrated model matches key untargeted moments related to frequency, severity, and the economy's behavior around financial crises.
  A well-known source of historical data on financial crises is the Macrohistory Database (Jorda, Schularick, and Taylor (2016, JST)). This database covers 17 advanced economies since 1870. According to this data set, systemic banking crises happen on average 4.0 times per 100 years. However, it is unclear whether the definition of “systemicness” used by JST immediately applies in my setting. For example, in many cases, their classification relies on Reinhart and Rogoff (2009), who define systemic crises as episodes associated with “closure, merging, or takeover by the public sector of one or more financial institutions.” That corresponds to all financial crises in the model. Using the OECD Economic Outlook reports, Romer and Romer (2017, RR) construct a more detailed measure of financial distress severity for 24 countries, including all countries in JST's sample, over a shorter period of time starting from 1967. Their measure suggests that the frequencies of systemic and nonsystemic crises are, respectively, 1.7% and 2.4% annually.21A subsample of JST's data over the same years implies an annual frequency of banking crises of 3.1%. Compared to JST, RR appear to identify more episodes of financial distress overall, although fewer of them are classified as systemic. See RR for a more detailed comparison of the chronologies.

However, despite its simplicity, the model is able to generate systemic crises whose frequency is more than two-thirds of that in the data.

A recent paper by Richter and Zimmermann (2021) constructs a novel data set on bank profitability covering 17 countries since 1870. It documents that bank profitability, as measured by the return on bank assets or equity, exhibits qualitatively similar behavior around systemic crises to that predicted by the model. It finds, in particular, that credit booms preceding systemic crises are initiated by increases in bank profitability. However, shortly before such crises, profitability declines.23
  An important model prediction is that portfolio commonality and systemic risk gradually increase prior to large-scale crises. Below, I explore whether this prediction holds in the data.
  Since the 2007–2008 crisis, numerous measures of systemic risk have been proposed in the literature (see Bisias et al. (2012) and Benoit et al. (2017) for reviews). article.IV. Welfare Analysis and Policy Implicat:
As shown in the previous section, systemic crises tend to happen when asset and bank lending amounts are above their long-run means. A natural question is why the representative household does not actively dissave in spite of high expected default costs. One reason is consumption smoothing. Second, taking the return on assets as given, the household does not internalize how its intertemporal asset accumulation decisions affect the productivity of risky projects. Being price takers, banks also do not internalize how their investments in the risky technology affect its productivity. Therefore, neither the household nor banks internalize that investment in the risky technology makes bank defaults more likely. Such a pecuniary externality has a nonzero welfare impact because of interbank market incompleteness and real default costs (Greenwald and Stiglitz (1986)). In this section, I solve the social planner's problem to quantify the importance of the pecuniary externality and discuss policy implications.Internet AppendixSectionIVcontains proofs for this section and provides additional details about the planner's problem.
  Before proceeding to the welfare analysis, it is worth noting that the benchmark model features another distortion. Specifically, banks are risk-neutral and do not weigh different states of the world by the household's stochastic discount factor. As a result, banks do not fully internalize utility losses associated with financial crises. The assumption of banks' risk neutrality substantially simplifies both theoretical and numerical analyses. InInternet AppendixSectionV, I solve the model without this assumption and show that the main results are similar and that the distortion due to misaligned preferences has a substantially smaller welfare impact than the distortion due to the pecuniary externality.
  Unlike the representative household, the planner internalizes how intertemporal savings decisions affect expected default losses. There are two main differences between theDE$DE$andSB$SB$asset accumulation policies. First, the planner dissaves more aggressively when facing elevated default risk. Figure8shows that, in comparison with theDE$DE$case, a typical systemic crisis in theSB$SB$economy is preceded by a smaller credit boom and caused by worse—and hence more extreme—realizations of aggregate and project-specific shocks.
  Second, theSB$SB$andDE$DE$economies feature different long-run steady states. On the one hand, the planner is willing to reduce the asset amount to keep the risky projects more productive and avoid painful systemic crises. On the other hand, nonsystemic crises become more likely under such conditions. Moreover, the planner also internalizes that diversification expenses are not deadweight losses and are eventually rebated to the household, so they do not dampen savings in theSB$SB$case.28While it is generally unclear which effect dominates, the amount of assets in theSB$SB$economy turns out to be on average 7.8% lower.
  The first two rows of TableIIIreportκDE→SB$\kappa ^{DE\rightarrow SB}$, along with the long-run averages of a few key variables for theDE$DE$andSB$SB$economies. Under the constrained planner, the asset amount, assets invested in the risky technology, consumption, labor input, and output are all lower. The risky technology is on average more productive, and investing banks have fewer incentives to diversify risks. Their portfolios are therefore less overlapping. The frequency of systemic events is more than halved compared to theDE$DE$allocation. This leads to a substantial welfare gain as such crises are associated with large real losses. Smaller scale financial crises are more frequent in theSB$SB$economy, but they are associated with much smaller real losses. The welfare gain from moving to theSB$SB$allocation is 0.11% of permanent consumption, comparable to existing estimates of the welfare cost of the pecuniary externality in other settings (e.g., Bianchi (2011)).
  TheSB$SB$allocation can be decentralized by a state-contingent taxτ(A,z,x)$\tau (A,z,x)$on the household's savingsahh′$a_{hh}^{\prime }$, with tax proceeds rebated to the household in a lump-sum fashion.29The optimal tax is set so that the planner's policies satisfy the representative household's intertemporal Euler equation.
  Proposition 2.A state-contingent taxτ(A,z,x)$\tau (A,z,x)$on the household's savings can restore constrained efficiency.
  
  
  Proof.SeeInternet AppendixSectionIV.A.□$\Box$
  
  I find that the optimal tax is positive on average (0.44%), equalizing the long-run steady states of theDE$DE$andSB$SB$economies. It is positively correlated withAand negatively correlated withzandx. Prior to systemic crises, the tax rate rises sharply (Figure9), preventing larger credit booms and higher expected default losses. In response to adverse realizations of project-specific shocks during systemic crises, τ drops and stays close to zero in the aftermath of systemic crises.
  Since state-contingent policies might be difficult to implement in practice, I also study the welfare implications of a simple flat tax. I find that the optimal fixed tax rate is 0.42%, quite close to the long-run average of the state-contingent tax. The welfare gain, however, is only about 55% of what is achieved by a time-varying policy (third row of TableIII). Unlike the state-contingent tax, the flat tax corrects the steady-state levels but, by definition, cannot prevent large buildups of credit and systemic risk.
  Finally, I investigate welfare implications of financial innovations that facilitate diversification, such as proliferation of securitization and credit risk transfer instruments or reduction in geographic barriers for banks. In the model, this corresponds to a decrease inf. There are two main effects on the economy. First, such innovations ease diversification. Holding everything else equal, this leads to a reduction in expected default losses, which is welfare-improving. However, it also pushes banks to increase investment in the risky technology (constraint (10) binds in more states of the world). Moreover, a lowerfimplies a higher return on the household's assets, which exacerbates the oversaving problem. As a result, welfare implications of a lower diversification cost are generally unclear.Internet AppendixSectionVIprovides a detailed analysis of how a change infaffects the economy. There I find, in particular, that the calibrated value off=0.0069$f=0.0069$is quite close to the optimal one.
  The policies considered in this section can improve welfare mainly because they reduce the frequency of large-scale financial crises. Although the optimal state-contingent tax does help the economy recover faster after systemic crises, it does not affect the immediate losses brought by breakdowns of the financial system. In this respect, interventions aimed at mitigating default losses, such as bailouts, might be desirable. Banks, rationally expecting such policies, are likely to change their behaviors ex ante (Acharya (2009), Farhi and Tirole (2012)). For example, within my framework, portfolios of investing banks are likely to become more correlated in expectation of broad-based bailouts after systemic crises. However, as suggested by recent work (Bianchi (2016), Allen et al. (2018)), the strength of a collective moral hazard problem might crucially depend on specific policy tools. A formal analysis of such interventions could be an interesting extension of this paper.
article.V. Conclusion:
This paper presents a general equilibrium model in which systemic risk arises due to time-varying commonality in banks' risk exposures. During periods of credit expansion, banks evenly spread funds across underlying projects, thereby reducing their individual bankruptcy probabilities but magnifying systemic risk. Episodes of large-scale financial distress tend to happen after credit booms, when productive investment opportunities are exhausted and bank portfolios are strongly correlated.
  To keep the analysis tractable, I consider the model in which banks internalize how their defaults affect the rest of the economy and, specifically, other parties within the financial sector. It would be interesting to analyze how externalities (e.g., due to fire sales, domino effects, or anticipated government interventions) affect banks' portfolio structure and systemic risk. Another interesting extension would be to introduce nontrivial heterogeneity across sectors. To focus on the diversification channel, I assume that the underlying projects are ex ante identical and differ only in realizations of nonpersistent shocks. In reality, project-specific shocks might be persistent and therefore may cause sectoral booms. Implications of such booms for credit allocation, banks' portfolio choice, and systemic risk are not well understood. These questions are left for future exploration.
